sources_audited.note: The May 2023 BLS industry table is for NAICS 541940 and reports employment shares and wages for veterinarians, veterinary technicians, veterinary assistants, animal caretakers, and office-support occupations; it also states that self-employed workers are excluded.
The O*NET veterinarian task page supports the cited examination, treatment, surgery, inoculation, specimen collection, diagnostic-equipment, counseling, advice, and administrative-task claims.
The O*NET technician record supports documentation, patient care, specimen and imaging work, equipment handling, close physical proximity, and serious consequences of error.

The AVMA report supports the stated practice evidence, including 40.8% owner enthusiasm toward new technology, veterinary services as the largest revenue category, and owners spending more than 65% of time with clients and patients. It does not directly measure AI realization or savings retention, which the packet correctly labels as estimates.

The BLS outlook supports veterinarian duties, 84% employment concentration in veterinary services, projected 10% employment growth from 2024 to 2034, and the stated pet-spending, treatment-option, and aging-pet demand drivers.

The AAHA article reports the 2018 Packaged Facts projection that 30% of veterinary practices would go up for sale during 2018-2028 and discusses buyer, size, geography, seller-stay, and slower-market constraints. The packet explicitly treats listings-to-transfers as a discounted proxy.
The Census NAICS profile is reachable and the official 2022 NAICS definition supports inclusion of licensed veterinary medicine, dentistry, surgery, and testing establishments.

caveats: The a estimate is a wage-weighted task judgment rather than a measured veterinary AI-exposure statistic; the occupational sources establish the work mix and task content but not the 0.28 base.
The rho estimate uses general enthusiasm toward new technology as contextual evidence, not direct evidence of AI deployment or sustained productivity in lower-middle-market practices.

The q source establishes direct veterinary-service revenue and owner time use but does not measure incidence or retention of AI savings; moreover, the entire registered q range maps to the C-factor cap of 10, so it expresses no factor-level sensitivity. This cannot plausibly change the current CONDITIONAL base tier or MODEL_CONDITIONED action under the registered score geometry.

The s5 proxy rests on an old ten-year projection of practices listed for sale rather than completed qualifying control transfers; the packet discloses and discounts that bridge, but direct lower-middle-market transaction evidence remains absent.

The d5 proxy converts veterinarian employment growth into constant-price, constant-quality service quantity even though employment also reflects productivity and occupational demand outside NAICS 541940; the mismatch is disclosed and the evidence state is appropriately PROXY.

The lens spans companion-animal, equine, food-animal, mixed, specialty, and testing businesses despite marking heterogeneity false. The narrative and primitive caveats acknowledge these differences, so this is a presentation caveat rather than undisclosed favorable lens selection.
summary: Publishable with caveats. Mechanics pass, S1-S7 are reachable and support the factual claims attributed to them, evidence states are honest, and the default industry lens is not shopped. The record is useful but heavily model-conditioned: AI opportunity and realization, eligibility, retention, and operator dependence are estimates, while transfer and demand inputs use disclosed proxies. No identified correction or evidence-state downgrade is shown to change the CONDITIONAL base tier or VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action, so there is no material finding under the brief's literal test.
